title: Geometry of arc spaces and the Hankel transform II
speaker: Ngô Bảo Châu

proof_stages.label: Braverman-Kazhdan local/global package
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the conjectural Schwartz space S_rho(G(F_v)), its basic function, the rho-Fourier transform by convolution with a kernel J_rho, the desired gamma-factor action on matrix coefficients, and the global Poisson summation formula.

proof_stages.label: Monoids, resolutions, and arc-space basic functions
proof_stages.summary: The known geometric input is organized through the reductive monoid M_rho, toric cones and possible resolutions of its singularities, with the basic function described by the IC complex on the formal arc space of M_rho.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field Fourier kernel from torus exponential sheaves
proof_stages.summary: Under a multiplicity-free assumption on rho, the kernel sheaf is constructed by restricting to the dual torus, pulling back an exponential sheaf along G_m^r, equipping it with W-equivariance, and inducing it to a perverse sheaf on G whose trace should give the correct gamma factors.

proof_stages.label: p-adic kernel and local-integrability conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The finite-field construction is transferred heuristically to p-adic fields by performing analogous regularized integrals over split and twisted tori, leading to a conjectural locally integrable function J_rho on regular semisimple elements that should preserve the Schwartz space and the basic vector.

proof_stages.label: Toric Hankel transform model
proof_stages.summary: For a torus, the expected Schwartz space is modeled as coinvariants of functions on affine space along the kernel torus, so the ordinary Fourier transform descends to the desired Hankel transform and suggests a toric Poisson/Voronoi formula.

proof_stages.label: Trace-formula reduction of global Poisson
proof_stages.summary: Because the direct Braverman-Kazhdan Poisson formula is difficult, the speaker proposes integrating it to obtain a trace formula and reducing the global identity to a local identity comparing orbital integrals before and after the rho-Hankel transform on maximal tori.

proof_stages.label: Orbital-integral tests and corrected local picture
proof_stages.summary: Computations for split tori support the orbital-integral identity via partition functions, while the GL2 nonsplit quadratic torus shows the naive identity fails and suggests decomposing orbital integrals into almost autodual pieces.

examples.name: Godement-Jacquet matrix model
examples.note: The standard representation of GL_n is used to make the conjectural Schwartz space, basic vector, and Fourier kernel concrete as functions on the matrix algebra and integral matrices.
examples.engagement_reason: the Godement-Jacquet matrix case is used as the standard model with its Schwartz space, basic function, and kernel stated but not derived or computed on

examples.name: T=G_m and the Kloosterman sheaf
examples.note: For a one-dimensional torus the pull-push of the exponential sheaf recovers the familiar Kloosterman sheaf, illustrating the finite-field construction of the kernel.
examples.engagement_reason: the case T=G_m is identified as giving the Kloosterman sheaf under the general push-pull construction, without working through that case specifically

examples.name: Split-torus partition function
examples.note: For split tori, the orbital integral of the basic function is computed as the weight-partition function counting decompositions into the weights of rho, showing that the naive torus identity has real content.
examples.engagement_reason: for a split torus the speaker computes/describes the basic function as the partition function counting expressions of a weight as a nonnegative sum of the representation weights

examples.name: GL2 standard representation and a nonsplit quadratic torus
examples.note: The GL2 standard case over a p-adic field shows that the naive local torus-by-torus identity fails, and the decomposition into pieces that are almost autodual suggests the corrected form of the local statement.
examples.engagement_reason: for GL2 with the standard representation and a nonsplit quadratic torus the speaker decomposes the orbital integral into characteristic-function pieces and traces how their Fourier transforms behave
